coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of curcumin-quaternized carbon quantum dots with enhanced broad-spectrum antibacterial activity for promoting infected wound healing
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Lina
Gao, Yaoran
Zhao, Chengfei
Huang, Dandan
Chen, Wenxin
Lin, Xinhua
Liu, Ailin
Lin, Liqing
coredata.dc:description: With the increasing incidence of multidrug-resistant antibacterial infections worldwide, developing new antibiotics to fight bacterial infections is urgent. The natural product curcumin has favorable antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects, but poor water solubility greatly limits its bioavailability, bioactivity and clinical application. Herein, to improve the bioactivity and enhance broad-spectrum antibacterial of curcumin, we synthesized quaternized carbon quantum dots (Q-CQDs) derived from the natural curcumin and 2,3-epoxypropyltrimethylammonium chloride (GTA) with highly solubility and stability by “double-thermal” method. It is proposed that the surfaces of Q-CQDs would still remain the active groups of curcumin and quaternary ammonium to boost the antibacterial activity. Experimental results reveal that the Q-CQDs possess excellent broad-spectrum antibacterial activity and the activity is significantly higher than that of natural curcumin. Investigation of the antibacterial mechanism of Q-CQDs showed that Q-CQDs functionalized with -N+(CH3)3 had strong adherence behavior on the bacterial cell membrane. Like a “Trojan Horse”, the bacterial cells lost their integrity, and the entry of Q-CQDs caused ROS generation and the efflux of cytoplasmic DNA and RNA, leading to the death of bacteria. The bacterial resistance of Q-CQDs was not observed, and Q-CQDs did not cause hemolysis and cytotoxicity. In vivo, the S. aureus-infected wounds, E. coli-infected wounds and mixed bacteria infected wounds healing tests with mice model indicate that Q-CQDs inhibited the bacterial population at the wound site, reduced inflammation and promoted wound healing. These results suggested that the Q-CQDs are a potential antibacterial candidate for clinical infected-wound healing applications and even bacteria resistant infections.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis and antibacterial properties of Q-CQDs and its therapeutic effect on infected wounds with bacteria.

(A)Physical appearance image of the curcumin (a) and Q-CQDs (b), (B)TEM image (inset: corresponding size distribution histogram), (C)HRTEM image, (D)the average hydrodynamic diameter and (E) the zeta potential of the Q-CQDs measured by DLS (n = 3).
XPS survey spectrum of Q-CQDs (A), and deconvolution of high-resolution analytical spectra of C1s (B), O1s (C) and N1s (D).

Antibacterial activity of curcumin (10 μg), GTA (50 μg) and Q-CQDs (10 μg) against the six species of bacteria on agar plates, including S. aureus, MRSA, S. epidermidis, E. faecalis, E. coli, and P. aeruginosa. Alkaline GTA was adjusted to neutral with hydrochloric acid solution (10 mmol/mL). Scale bars equal to 20 mm.

TEM images of S. aureus (A and E), MRSA (B and F), S. epidermidis (C and G) and E. coli (D and H) with the treatment of normal saline (A, B, C and D as control groups) and Q-CQDs (E, F, G and H as experimental groups). Scale bars equal to 200 nm.
Propidium iodide (PI) staining assays of S. aureus and E. coli after 4 h incubation with Q-CQDs at 0.5 mg/mL via CLSM. The scale bar equals 5 μm.

Fluorescence spectra of adding Q-CQDs and H2O2 into the S. aureus (A) and E. coli (B) solution containing DCFH-DA, respectively. The cytoplasmic DNA and RNA efflux after adding Q-CQDs into the S. aureus and E. coli solution (C). The main mechanism of the antibacterial effect of Q-CQDs (D).

Resistance studies of Q-CQDs and levofloxacin hydrochloride toward S. aureus (A) and E. coli (B), (C) Hemolytic activities of Q-CQDs (5, 10, 25, 50, 100, 200 and 500 μg/mL) solutions to RBCs in Saline. Hemolysis assay with Saline and DI water as negative control group and positive control group, respectively. Insets: photographs of corresponding RBC solutions, (D) Relative viabilities of 293 T and Hep G2 cells treated with different concentrations of Q-CQDs for 24 h. Error bars represent the standard deviation of three repeated measurements.

Photographs of healing process of the S. aureus-infected wounds (A), E. coli-infected wounds (B) and S. aureus-co-E. coli-infected wounds (C) in group treated with normal saline, Q-CQDs group, and Levofloxacin group. Schematic diagram of infection and treatment process (D) and photographs of the amounts of bacterial colonies in the tissues after infected 48 h and treating 14 days in S. aureus-infected wounds, E. coli-infected wounds and S. aureus-co-E. coli infected-wounds (E).

H&E-stained histological sections of the middle tissues of the wound (local view), including before infection (A, F and K), infection for 48 h (B, G and L) and treatment for 14 days (C, D and E belong to S. aureus, H, I and J belong to E. coli, M, N and O belong to and S. aureus-co-E. coli); Scale bars equal to 200 μm. Blue arrows indicate the neutrophils, and red arrows indicate the connective tissue. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Synthesis of curcumin-quaternized carbon quantum dots with enhanced broad-spectrum antibacterial activity for promoting infected wound healing

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With the increasing incidence of multidrug-resistant antibacterial infections worldwide, developing new antibiotics to fight bacterial infections is urgent. The natural product curcumin has favorable antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects, but poor water solubility greatly limits its bioavailability, bioactivity and clinical application. Herein, to improve the bioactivity and enhance broad-spectrum antibacterial of curcumin, we synthesized quaternized carbon quantum dots (Q-CQDs) derived from the natural curcumin and 2,3-epoxypropyltrimethylammonium chloride (GTA) with highly solubility and stability by “double-thermal” method. It is proposed that the surfaces of Q-CQDs would still remain the active groups of curcumin and quaternary ammonium to boost the antibacterial activity. Experimental results reveal that the Q-CQDs possess excellent broad-spectrum antibacterial activity and the activity is significantly higher than that of natural curcumin. Investigation of the antibacterial mechanism of Q-CQDs showed that Q-CQDs functionalized with -N+(CH3)3 had strong adherence behavior on the bacterial cell membrane. Like a “Trojan Horse”, the bacterial cells lost their integrity, and the entry of Q-CQDs caused ROS generation and the efflux of cytoplasmic DNA and RNA, leading to the death of bacteria. The bacterial resistance of Q-CQDs was not observed, and Q-CQDs did not cause hemolysis and cytotoxicity. In vivo, the S. aureus-infected wounds, E. coli-infected wounds and mixed bacteria infected wounds healing tests with mice model indicate that Q-CQDs inhibited the bacterial population at the wound site, reduced inflammation and promoted wound healing. These results suggested that the Q-CQDs are a potential antibacterial candidate for clinical infected-wound healing applications and even bacteria resistant infections.

ce:section.ce:para: Infectious problems caused by multiple bacteria have threated the health and life of public in the 21st century [1]. Infectious problems are facing the complicated situation of multiple sources and multiple factors, including the pathogen, human body, and the surrounding environment. Once the diseases caused by mixed infection are prevalent, they often cause high morbidity and mortality, causing huge economic losses to the national economy. In clinical treatment of mixed infection, appropriate antibacterial drugs should be selected according to the drug sensitivity results of laboratories, and broad-spectrum antibacterial drugs often be used to control the disease condition at the same time. However, due to the difficulty of identification of some pathogens and drug resistance to some antibiotics, the treatment cycle is prolonged and even the mutation of drug-resistant bacteria is intensified.

With the rapid prevalence of antibiotic resistance, the research of new antibacterial agents has been concerned. Recently, nanoparticles (NP) have been extensively studied and shown excellent potential for biomedical applications [2–9], especially have good prospect in antibacterial researches [2–5]. Among many NP-based antimicrobials, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) belonging to zero-dimensional nanomaterials have unique shape and size, surface roughness [7,8], surface functional groups and obvious advantages in water solubility, modifiability and good biocompatibility [5,10]. The surface of nanomaterials can form wrinkles or striation structures, which can significantly change the properties of carbon source, such as hydrophobicity, transparency, surface area, electrical conductivity, etc. [7]. The nanomaterials with wrinkles or striation structures on surface always have cytotoxicity compared with CQDs with small size [10]. Therefore, CQDs are increasingly applied in the field of biomedical engineering, sensor technology [6], photocatalysis [9] and the drug delivery for disease treatment [11,12]. However, the research of antibacterial CQDs is still in the preliminary stage [13]. We have developed quaternary ammonium CQDs for Gram-positive bacteria inactivation [14]. Yang et al. have developed silicon CQDs for Gram-positive bacteria inactivation and imaging [15]. Because traditional natural products have the advantages of antibacterial activities, small side effects and low drug resistance [16,17]. Several natural products have antibacterial function, e.g., curcumin [18], chitosan [19] and others [20]. Accumulated evidences suggest that curcumin is a multi-functional compound that has antioxidant [21], anti-inflammatory [22], anticancer [23,24], anti-HIV [25], and broad-spectrum antibacterial properties [18], etc. However, the bioavailability of curcumin is still a problem to be solved [26], which is mainly improved by modifying curcumin structure or conjugation [27]. Cheng et al. prepared the nanocurcumin, which increased the concentration of curcumin in plasma and residence time in mouse brain [28]. Wong S et al. found that the sugar has the ability to guide self-assembly of curcumin to form well-defined capsules with non-covalent bonds [29]. These studies indicate that the conjugation and structural modifications can improve the bioavailability of curcumin for further application. How to promote the better play of curcumin pharmacological activity is a very worthy of research topics. Therefore, it is of concern whether CQDs derived from the natural product curcumin has broad-spectrum antibacterial activity and enhanced solubility.

Quaternary ammonium compounds endow them with more special properties, such as colorless, odorless, non-toxic, non-irritating to skin, good water solubility, and excellent surface activity. They are widely used as disinfectants, surfactants, fabric softeners, and antistatic agents. With the increasing antibiotic resistance of bacteria, quaternary ammonium salt-based antibacterial compounds have gradually attracted widespread attention. The carbon dots modified by surface quaternization with benzalkonium chloride, which has improved water solubility and engrossing antibacterial activity against E. coli [30]. Our group has developed quaternary ammonium CQDs derived from GTA and diallyldimethylammonium chloride by one-pot method for bacteria inactivation. However, it has antibacterial effects only on Gram-positive bacteria [14].

As reported in the literatures, there are several mechanisms of the antibacterial effects of CQDs, such as physical or mechanical damage [31], reactive oxygen species (ROS)-induced oxidative stress [32], photo-dependent effect [33,34] and DNA or RNA interference [35], etc. Recent reports show that the CQDs kill bacteria with the addition of H2O2 [36] or photo-stimulation [33,37,38] to produce ROS. Unfortunately, extra stimulation can also cause irreversible damage to normal tissues. Therefore, the antibacterial CQDs without extra stimulation and can be processed under various situations is highly desired.

Herein, we address the challenge of broad-spectrum antimicrobial. This work will use curcumin and GTA as raw materials to synthesize Q-CQDs. The preparation process of the Q-CQDs, the application in antibacterial field and the process of wound healing were shown in Fig. 1 . The obtained Q-CQDs has several charming capabilities: (1) The Q-CQDs organically combines quaternary ammonium N functional groups with positively charges to give full play on their functions through adhering to bacteria to realize bacteria killing. (2) The Q-CQDs has broad-spectrum antibacterial activity and inhibition against MRSA with MIC of 8 μg/mL. (3) The antimicrobial mechanism of Q-CQDs can be attributed to the strong adhesion behavior of the -N+(CH3)3 functionalized Q-CQDs on the bacterial cell membrane and lost the integrity of the bacterial cells. The permeation of Q-CQDs into microorganisms caused ROS generation and the efflux of cytoplasmic DNA and RNA. (4) Through the mouse skin wound and the main organ toxicity test, the results showed that Q-CQDs could promote wound healing quickly and exhibited good biocompatibility. The novelty of our manuscript was that the prepared Q-CQDs possess excellent broad-spectrum antibacterial activity, no acquired drug resistance, and could inhibit the bacterial population of infected wound, reduce inflammation and promote wound healing.

The Q-CQDs preparation was utilized a mixed type of hydrothermal and microwave method. The specific process was as follows: After mixing the suspension including a 0.1 g of curcumin and 0.5 g of GTA and 10 mL ultrapure water and transferring it into 50 mL autoclave. And then, the suspension was at 200 °C pyrolytic reaction for 24 h and the color of the liquid was gradually changed to brownish black after reaction. After then, the stock solution was added to 10 mL MilliQ water at 80 °C for 30 min with power input of 500 W. Before further used, the raw Q-CQDs solution was centrifuged, filtered with 0.22 μm microporous membrane and dialyzed with dialysis membrane (MWCO 100-500) for 24 h. After purification, the Q-CQDs stock solution was freeze-dried and weighed to calculate the product yield of Q-CQDs. Finally, the Q-CQDs powders were stored at 4 °C for the subsequent tests.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bacteria culture and assays of antibacterial activity in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: Bacteria culture: All bacteria were cultured at 37 °C for 24 h and inoculated into sterile saline solution (0.9% NaCl) to prepare the bacterial suspension at wavelength of 600 nm (OD600) [39]. The OD600 value of bacterial suspension was about 0.1 ± 0.02 and was used for the subsequent tests.

Assays of antibacterial activity: Bacteriostatic curves [39], disk diffusion method [40], broth dilution method [41], TEM characterization [42], dead/live bacteria staining [43] were carried out as reported.

Animal experiments were guided of guideline and approved of the Animal Care and Use Committee of Fujian Medical University (No. FJMU IACUC 2019-0119). Institute of Cancer Research (ICR) mice (100 mice，27 ± 2.5 g) were employed in the experiments. 90 ICR mice were randomly divided into control group (30 mice), Levofloxacin hydrochloride (30 mice) and Q-CQDs group (30 mice). After anesthesia with isoflurane, the wounds of ICR mice were resected with a scalpel blade (10.0 mm in diameter, Fig. S1) [44]. After the above operation, 2 mL of 1.5 × 108 CFU/mL bacterial suspension was coated to the wounds. During the treatment, the groups were treated once a day with normal saline, Levofloxacin hydrochloride and Q-CQDs, respectively. On the 3th, 5th, 7th, 10th and 14th days, the recovery of wound surfaces in each group were investigated, compared, photographed. Meanwhile, the section of wound tissues in each group were homogenized and diluted to place on LB agar medium [45]. In vivo toxicity experiences (10 mice), 0.5 mL/100 g of 2 mg/mL Q-CQDs solution was used to inject into mice intraperitoneally for 7 days continuously for the subsequent tests. The tissues of the mice above were collected and stained with hematoxylin-eosin (H&E). The images of H&E staining were scanned on a pathological scanner (KF-PRO-005-EX, China).

To explore the viability of Q-CQDs, methyl thiazolyl tetrazolium (MTT) assay [45] was carried out. 293 T, HepG2, B16F1 and Bewo cells were seeded in DMEM and incubated with the Q-CQDs (0, 5, 10, 20, 40 and 80 μg/mL) for 24 h. After then, the MTT solution (0.5 mg/mL) was added, cultured and replaced with dimethyl sulfoxide to measure the OD490 nm on a microplate reader with Skanlt Software 3.2 (Multiskan GO, Thermo Scientific). Meanwhile, the optical images of different cells (including 293 T, Hep G2, B16F1 and Bewo cell) with the Q-CQDs (0, 5, 10, 20, 40 and 80 μg/mL) during cytotoxicity measurements were investigated to verify the nontoxicity of Q-CQDs by inverted fluorescence microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of bacterial resistance to Q-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Evaluation of bacterial resistance to Q-CQDs: The initial MIC values of control levofloxacin hydrochloride and Q-CQDs against S. aureus and E. coli were obtained as described above. Bacteria from 96-well plate at the concentration of one-half MIC were then used to prepare the bacterial dilution (about 107 CFU/mL) for the next MIC detection. These bacterial suspensions were then incubated with control levofloxacin hydrochloride (levo-HCl) and Q-CQDs, respectively. The new MIC values were obtained after incubation at 37 °C for 18 h. The MIC for each passage until 18 passages were recorded and compared with the first passage to determine the drug resistance enhancement.

The hemolysis assay [46] was performed with patient's consent (the First Affiliated Hospital of Fujian Medical University). The erythrocytes were centrifugated and washed to prepare the stock dispersion at 20%. After then, red blood cell (RBC) with or without Q-CQDs solution (0, 5, 25, 50, 100, 200 and 500 μg/mL) were prepared and incubated for 3 h at 37 °C. Finally, after centrifugated, the OD540 nm of supernatant were measured by UV–vis analysis. The percentage of hemolysis was calculated as the formula:

where OD540 Q-CQDs, OD540 blank and OD540 DI water is the OD540 values in Q-CQDs, normal saline and DI water with the erythrocyte suspension.

Intracellular ROS generation [47] and cytoplasmic DNA and RNA efflux [48] was measured to explore the antibacterial mechanism of Q-CQDs. S. aureus and E. coli were exposed to different concentration Q-CQDs solutions (1 and 10 μg/mL) to evaluate intracellular ROS production and bacterial cell membrane integrity (DNA and RNA). Briefly, S. aureus or E. coli bacterial suspension was incubated with Q-CQDs solution for 2 h at 37 °C. After then, S. aureus and E. coli cells were collected, washed twice, resuspended in normal saline and incubated with DCFH-DA (2 mL, 10 mM) at 37 °C for 30 min. The fluorescence intensity (λex/λem = 488 nm/525 nm) was measured for assessing ROS generation after incubated with S. aureus and E. coli cells. Meanwhile, the mixture without DCFH-DA was then simultaneously filtered (0.22 μm) and the supernatant of DNA and RNA was quantified with an ND-2000 spectrophotometer (NanoDrop) at 260 nm.

All data were acquired with parallel experiment (n = 3). All statistical analyses were performed with Student's t-test using GraphPad Prism 8. P < 0.05 was considered as a statistically significant difference.

The prepared Q-CQDs were characterized and further against different bacteria. The study plan aimed to demonstrate the in vitro and in vivo antibacterial ability and cytotoxicity experiments for evaluation of broad-spectrum antibacterial effects and biosafety of Q-CQDs.

Q-CQDs were synthesized via two steps hydrothermal and microwave method using curcumin and GTA as raw materials (Fig. 1). The as-prepared Q-CQDs has highly water-dispersible as deep yellow-brown solution (Fig. 2A). The 2 mg/mL curcumin aqueous solution was undissolved because the aqueous insolubility of natural curcumin and the heterogeneity of the suspension. The dimensional change of nanomaterials can influence the physical characteristics and the antibacterial activity [49]. Formation of Q-CQDs was attributed to the carbonization of small-sized curcumin molecular [50]. The morphology and size of the Q-CQDs were characterized in Fig. 2B. The transmission electron microscope (TEM) image of Q-CQDs showed the nearly monodisperse sphericity had similar average particle sizes of 3.05 ± 0.95 nm (Fig. 2B; count from 100 counts for each in Fig. 2B). The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images of Q-CQDs with d-spacing values of 0.22 nm was correspond to the diffraction planes of (100) facets of graphite (Fig. 2C) [51]. Q-CQDs were measured by Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) with the average hydrodynamic diameter at 4 ± 0.7 nm (Fig. 2D) and the zeta potential at +10 ± 0.5 mV (Fig. 2E). The Q-CQDs showed negligible variation in average hydrodynamic diameter and zeta potential in the different culture media (Fig. S2) and stabilized within 90 days (Fig. S3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of surface groups of Q-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Through the UV–vis spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy of curcumin, GTA and Q-CQDs, the functional groups, and optical property of them were investigated. GTA showed a weaker absorption peak at 265 nm and a strong terminal absorption. Natural curcumin showed typical absorption peaks at 263 nm and 425 nm. The Q-CQDs produced the absorption peak at 290 nm and the curcumin typical absorption peaks at 425 nm disappeared (Fig. S4A). Q-CQDs produced an emission fluorescence spectrum at 470 nm and a strong blue fluorescence under the wavelength of 365 nm (Fig. S4B and inset). Meanwhile, Q-CQDs exhibit excitation-dependent emission properties (Fig. S4C). The 3.3% quantum yield of Q-CQDs was calculated by using quinine sulfate in 0.1 mol/L H2SO4 (quantum yield is 54% at 350 nm) as a reference at optimized excitation wavelength 365 nm (Fig. S4D). The UV–vis spectrum showed the similar spectral shape of Q-CQDs through single hydrothermal method and hydrothermal assisted with microwave “double-thermal” method (Fig. S5), indicating the similar structures of them. However, the UV absorption intensity of Q-CQDs increased by the “double-thermal” method was attributed the microwave assistant [52]. The UV absorption increased was attributed to the better carbonized with the increase of carbonization time, carbonization temperatures or mixing degree, i.e., which ultimately resulted in better dots states.

XPS was measured to determine the four elements carbon (C), nitrogen (N), oxygen (O) and chlorine (Cl) in Q-CQDs. Fig. 3A was the XPS spectrum of Q-CQDs, which showed the percentages of C, N, O and Cl in Q-CQDs are 72.42%, 6.96%, 17.68% and 2.94%, respectively. The valence bonds of the three elements C, N and O and the groups contained in Q-CQDs were further analyzed. The absorption peaks of C1s at 286.5 eV and 284.8 eV correspond to C-OH single-bond and C-C/C-N single-bond /C=C double-bond [53] (Fig. 3B). The O1s analysis results showed the three absorption peaks at 531.9 eV, 532.8 eV and 533.5 eV positions were generated by O=C-N double-bond, the O=C-OH double-bond and C-O-H single-bond (sp2 hybrid oxygen) (Fig. 3C). The N1s had absorption peaks at 402.2 eV, corresponding to -N+(CH3)3, indicating that N was doped into Q-CQDs in quaternary ammonium group [54]. The Cl2p was consisted of the 2p1/2 and 2p3/2 transition modes of CCl single-bond at 197.72 eV and 199.23 eV [55] (Fig. S6).

The functional groups of Q-CQDs were characterized by FTIR (Fig. S7). The absorption peaks of phenolic hydroxyl groups at 3414 cm−1 appeared either in curcumin or Q-CQDs, indicating that the synthetic reaction of Q-CQDs did not involve in the -OH groups. Since the -OH groups in the curcumin might play important roles of antibacterial activity, anti-oxidant and radicals scavenging [21], indicating that the -OH groups on the Q-CQDs might be contributed to antibacterial activity. The asymmetric CH stretching of -OCH3 groups at 2920 cm−1 appeared in curcumin and Q-CQDs. The absorption peaks of Q-CQDs and GTA at 3018 cm−1 both were generated by asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibration of CH single-bond of -N+(CH3)3 [56]. The absorption peak of Q-CQDs at 964 cm−1 and 1637 cm−1 were attributed to the CC and CC bond stretching vibration, which were a typical graphene carbon (CC /C=C) structure. The absorption peak of Q-CQDs at 1481 cm−1 was generated by CH single-bond scissor in-plane bending vibration linked to -N+(CH3)3, which indicated that the Q-CQDs had been successfully quaternized [57]. Therefore, the CN, -OH, -OCH3 and -N+(CH3)3 were retained in Q-CQDs by the introduction of GTA into Q-CQDs and retention of active groups of curcumin.

Proton (1H) and carbon (13C) standard NMR experiments of the curcumin, GTA and Q-CQDs were measured and allowed to gather determinant information about the chemical structure and connectivity. In Fig. S8A, the comparison of the 1H spectra of curcumin, GTA and Q-CQDs were shown. The NMR profiles of GTA and Q-CQDs were remarkably similar, showing that the same type of chemical structure (-N+(CH3)3) was obtained from GTA. It indicated successful the conjugation of GTA to curcumin, which could obtain the Q-CQDs by the covalent binding of CN according to the results of XPS and FTIR. In detail, the two peaks at 2.67 and 3.53 ppm of GTA disappeared in Q-CQDs, which meant the epoxy group was reacted with curcumin under high temperature. Meanwhile, the absence of the aromatic ring and methyl of curcumin (δ 7.56, 7.18 ppm) and the methyl of GTA (δ 3.34 ppm) and the arise of new methyl and resonance signals of the aromatic ring at δ 3.63 and 3.98 ppm, suggested the formation of Q-CQDs. Besides these signals, the single peak at 2.83 ppm attributed to methylene group in NCH2 could also be seen in Q-CQDs, which might be that the part of GTA coexisted with aromatic region in the reaction and be present in the core from the curcumin carbon source after the thermal treatment. In addition, the 13C NMR spectra in Fig. S8B showed that there were no initial reactants (curcumin and GTA) in the as-prepared Q-CQDs. However, the separation purification of Q-CQDs to obtain the effective constituent of antimicrobial activities to be further studied in the future. According to FTIR, XPS and 1H/13C NMR analysis results, it was inferred that Q-CQDs contain functional groups including -OH, -OCH3, -N+(CH3)3, CO, CO and typical graphene carbon (C-C/C=C), which could boost the antibacterial activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro antibacterial activity of Q-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The disk diffusion test was enforced to explore the antibacterial effect of Q-CQDs (Fig. 4 ). Disk contained 10 μg curcumin (a, Fig. 4), 50 μg GTA (b, Fig. 4) and 10 μg Q-CQDs (c, Fig. 4) on MHA plates, respectively. The bacteriostatic circles were not formed around disk a and b of all MHA plates for the reaction substrates curcumin and GTA had no antibacterial effect. Therefore, the antibacterial spectrum of Q-CQDs was expanded and antibacterial effect was increased, compared with curcumin and GTA. What's more, the obvious bacteriostatic circle was formed around the paper sheet of the MHA plates incubated with Q-CQDs. However, inhibition zones were obviously different formed on MHA plates incubated with different bacteria because different bacteria had different characteristic that had influenced the antibacterial activity of Q-CQDs. Therefore, the Q-CQDs had broad-spectrum antibacterial effect and were more effective on Gram-positive bacteria. Furthermore, the antibacterial stability of Q-CQDs was also evaluated by disk diffusion test, as shown in Fig. S9. The Q-CQDs (100 μg, Fig. S9) stored at different time (1, 2, 3, 4, 5 and 6 months) were put in the MHA plates to observe the bacteriostatic circle of S. aureus, MRSA and E. coli, which showed the similar size and the excellent antibacterial stability. Therefore, the results denoted that Q-CQDs possess broad-spectrum antibacterial activity and 6-months storage did not compromise the antibacterial activities of Q-CQDs.

The MIC of curcumin, GTA and Q-CQDs against different bacteria were determined by constant broth dilution test (Table S1). According to the results in the table, the MIC of curcumin and GTA to the above six bacteria were greater than 320 μg/mL, which suggested there are no antibacterial activity for curcumin and GTA in the solution of MH broth. However, the MIC of Q-CQDs to S. epidermidis was the smallest, at 4 μg/mL, the MIC to S. aureus and MRSA were larger, at 8 μg/mL, and the MIC to E. faecalis, E. coli, and P. aeruginosa were 16, 32, 64 μg/mL, respectively. In addition, the zone diameter of different bacterial inhibition of Q-CQDs from the disk diffusion test were 17 ± 3 mm on MHA plates, which were consist with the broth dilution test. Q-CQDs had a broad-spectrum antimicrobial activity, which greatly improved the antibacterial effect compared with curcumin. The time-dependent bacteriostasis of Q-CQDs on above bacteria, were carried out at different concentrations by measuring the OD600 (Fig. S10). The OD600 values were significantly lower in the bacteria with Q-CQDs at different concentrations compared with the ones without Q-CQDs, indicating that Q-CQDs have excellent inhibitory effects on the six selected bacterial species. The Q-CQDs showed different OD600 values for different bacteria, suggesting different degrees of inhibition of Q-CQDs. And that the MIC results of the micro broth method for different bacteria were consistent with the MIC of Q-CQDs in the constant broth dilution test in Table S1.

The destruction of bacterial cells by Q-CQDs was characterized by TEM (Fig. 5 ). After S. aureus, MRSA, S. epidermidis and E. coli cultured with normal saline, the complete bacterial structures (cell walls were clearly visible and the density of intracellular substances was uniform) was retained (Fig. 5A, B, C and D). However, after bacteria were cultured with Q-CQDs, the bacteria disintegrated in different degrees (bacterial walls and membranes were obviously ruptured, intracellular substances leaked out and agglomerated, bacteria lost integrity). The irreversible damage to thallus by Q-CQDs was similar with other antibacterial materials reported in the literature [42]. Because the damage of Q-CQDs to bacterial membrane was physical, which entering the cell with the positive charge so that it was not easy to be discharged out and hardly generated bacterial drug resistance. Therefore, compared with traditional antibiotics, Q-CQDs had obvious antibacterial advantages.

In order to further study the destruction of the membrane of bacteria by Q-CQDs, the propidium iodide (PI) and Syto9 staining experiments were implemented for the observation of dead/live bacteria after incubation with Q-CQDs (Figs. 6 and S11). After S. aureus and E. coli in normal saline was stained with PI, there were almost no fluorescence, which indicated that most bacteria were alive. However, after 4 h of treatment with Q-CQDs, the bacteria showed strong red fluorescence, indicating that Q-CQDs could effectively kill bacteria. At the same time, after S. aureus and E. coli in normal saline were stained with Syto9, they showed strong green fluorescence, which indicated that most of the bacteria were alive (Fig. S11). After being treated with Q-CQDs for 4 h, there was almost no fluorescence, indicating that plenty of bacteria died, further indicating that Q-CQDs could effectively kill bacteria. Therefore, these observations indicated that Q-CQDs had the ability to effectively destroy the structure of bacterial membranes.

Quaternary ammonium compounds generate ROS to damage the intracellular substances and kill bacteria. The level of ROS production was studied by using DCFH-DA assay and the results was shown in Fig. 7 A, B. H2O2 (ROS representative substance) generated ROS in S. aureus and E. coli suspension, which transferred DCF (with fluorescence) from DCFH-DA (without fluorescence). Q-CQDs (1 and 10 μg/mL) treatment for 30 min also induced pronounced the ROS generation in S. aureus and E. coli suspension, which showed Q-CQDs induced the generation of intracellular ROS with the increase of Q-CQDs. Moreover, the zeta potential of Q-CQDs was +10 mV (Fig. S12), which indicated the electrostatic interaction between Q-CQDs and bacterial membranes led to the perforation and rupture of membrane and the death of bacteria [58]. In addition, as the nucleic acids (DNA and RNA) of bacteria were monitored after incubated with Q-CQDs (Fig. 7C). The results showed that the concentration of released nucleic acids were increased at 2.11 and 2.01 ng/μL with Q-CQDs treatment in S. aureus and E. coli suspension, respectively. These results further verified that Q-CQDs had the ability of membrane disruption. Combining the above results, the Q-CQDs first destroyed the integrity of the bacteria, produced the ROS and then disordered from the inside, resulting in bacterial death (Fig. 7D). Taken together, these results of the antibacterial mechanism of Q-CQDs suggested that -N+(CH3)3 functionalized Q-CQDs had strong adherence behavior on the bacterial cell membrane. Like a “Trojan Horse”, Q-CQDs can disrupt the integrity of the membrane, and the entry of Q-CQDs caused ROS generation and the efflux of cytoplasmic DNA and RNA, leading to the death of bacteria.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Resistance study and safety investigation of the Q-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate whether the Q-CQDs could gradually induce bacterial resistance, S. aureus and E. coli at sub-inhibitory concentrations were cultured with Q-CQDs to detect MIC values for 18 passages. Levofloxacin hydrochloride with broad-spectrum fluoroquinolones was used as a positive control, and parallel cultures were exposed to serial dilutions. For levofloxacin hydrochloride, the MICs against S. aureus and E. coli was increased by passage 8 and 6, respectively, and the MICs of the Q-CQDs did not change significantly within 18 passages (Fig. 8A, B). The results indicated that it is not particularly easy for S. aureus and E. coli to evolve resistance to the Q-CQDs. Q-CQDs did not lead to bacterial resistance, which should be because the antimicrobial mechanism of Q-CQDs was through electrostatic contact and then enters the bacteria to stimulate intracellular ROS production. The interacting behavior of Q-CQDs through electrostatic binding rather than specifically on bacterial subunits may not induce the bacterial acquisition antimicrobial resistance evolution. Therefore, the antibacterial property without acquired drug resistance implied that Q-CQDs may be an effective and promising strategy for antibacterial capability.

The safety investigation of the Q-CQDs in vitro including hemolysis and cytotoxicity experiments. Hemolysis rate of Q-CQDs was calculated to explore whether it could cause the erythrolysis. As showed in Fig. 8C, the red blood cells were ruptured in DI water but intact in sterilized physiological saline solution with or without different concentrations of Q-CQDs. The hemolysis rate of Q-CQDs at 500 μg/mL was calculated to only 4.5% (<5%), which indicated that Q-CQDs had good blood compatibility. The cytotoxicity of Q-CQDs on 293 T (normal cells), Hep G2, B16F1 and Bewo cells (overgrowth cells) were evaluated by MTT test. Fig. 8D showed that the survival rate of 293 T and Hep G2 cells were still more than 80% with the concentration of Q-CQDs reached 80 μg/mL (far above the MIC) within 24 h, which showed that Q-CQDs had low toxicity to 293 T and Hep G2 cells in vitro. Furthermore, the survival rate of B16F1 and Bewo cell were more than 80% at 80 μg/mL with the Q-CQDs after 24 h incubation as showed in Fig. S13. Meanwhile, the optical images of different cells (including 293 T, Hep G2, B16F1 and Bewo cell) with Q-CQDs at different concentrations for 24 h during cytotoxicity measurements were provided to verify the nontoxicity (Fig. S14). The distribution of viable and dead cells can be investigated visually using calcein AM (green fluorescence) and propidium iodide (PI, red fluorescence). Different cells were treated with different concentrations of Q-CQDs (0, 5, 10, 20, 40 and 80 μg/mL) in a culture medium for 24 h. Then, living and dead cells were stained with calcein AM and PI to produce green or red fluorescence, respectively. Images obtained by fluorescent inverted microscope revealed that no significant damage was evident in the control and Q-CQDs group, respectively. The findings demonstrated that Q-CQDs had outstanding cytocompatibility. The desirable results may be that these cells contain more cholesterol in the plasma membrane to enhance the cell membrane integrity, and they are more resistant to the damaging potential of Q-CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo therapeutic effect of Q-CQDs on infected wound
ce:section.ce:para: The antibacterial effect of the Q-CQDs in vivo was showed in Fig. 9 . The healing process of the S. aureus-infected wounds (A), E. coli-infected wounds (B) and S. aureus-co-E. coli-infected wounds (C), suggested the Q-CQDs had the antibacterial activities in different bacteria-infected animal model, both single infection and cross infection. Infection and treatment process was showed in Fig. 9D. The infected wounds were filled with the massive abscess after bacteria-infected for 48 h. After treatment for 5 days, the obvious scab appeared with the treatment of Levofloxacin hydrochloride or Q-CQDs, whereas the negative group still was imbued with pus and exudates. After treatment for 10 days, almost complete recovery and no fester or hypertrophic scarring was observed and the fur started to repair. After treatment for 14 days, the weight of each group mice decreased after infected and hadobvious weight increase during the treatment process at enough supply (Fig. S15A, B and C). Meanwhile, the wound areas in the saline, Levofloxacin hydrochloride and Q-CQDs groups were calculated to be 13.83 ± 1.49 %, 0.17 ± 0.07 % and 0.14 ± 0.07 % (S. aureus), 12.72 ± 0.55 %, 0.51 ± 0.09 % and 0.38% ± 0.14 (E. coli), 12.80 ± 2.67 %, 0.52 ± 0.18 % and 0.24 ± 0.15 % (S. aureus-co-E. coli) (Fig. S15D, E and F), respectively. In addition, the amounts of bacteria of the obtained infected wound tissues were homogenized and diluted to place on MH agar medium. In detail, the photographs (Fig. 9E) showed the amounts of bacterial colonies in the tissues after infected 48 h and treating 14 days in S. aureus-infected wounds, E. coli-infected wounds and S. aureus-co-E. coli infected-wounds. Numerous colonies existed in the negative group, but were absent in the experimental and positive controls. Therefore, Q-CQDs were as effective as Levofloxacin hydrochloride in the treatment of wounds infected with S. aureus, E. coli and S. aureus-co-E. coli through killing bacteria and promoting infected-wounds healing. The significant reduction of the bacteria colonies in infected tissues is mainly attributed to the sustained antibacterial activity of Q-CQDs. Therefore, the prepared Q-CQDs may be a potential candidate for controlling infection with excellent antibacterial efficiency.

The photomicrographs of H&E staining of infected-wound tissues were observed in local (Fig. 10 ) and panorama (Fig. S16) view. In infected wounds, the microscopic inflammatory response (blue arrow in Fig. 10B, G, L) was consistent with the pus and exudates of the infected wounds through the naked eyes. Meanwhile, the demarcation line and continuous tissue was absent (red arrow in Fig. 10B, G, L). After 14 days of treatment with Q-CQDs, the intact layer of epithelialization and dense connective tissue (red arrow in Fig. 10D, E, I, J, N and O) was present and the inflammatory response disappeared (blue arrow in Fig. 10D, E, I, J, N and O), which corroborated the intense healing of the wound. However, the inflammation in the negative control group was still in infected state (Fig. 10C, H and M). The above results indicated that the granulation tissue, epithelial regeneration, angiogenesis and collagen fibers were significantly repaired at the end of healing of the wound infected by S. aureus, E. coli and S. aureus-co-E. coli in the Levofloxacin hydrochloride and Q-CQDs treated animals.

The in vivo toxicity of Q-CQDs was explored for further applications. After the mice were treated with Q-CQDs, they did not suffer from weight loss, diet reduction and mental depression. The pathological sections of the heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney stained with H&E showed no significant difference in the tissue structure of the Q-CQDs group compared with that of the blank mice (Fig. S17), which meant that Q-CQDs did not cause pathological damage in vivo. It was also implied that Q-CQDs could be converted into harmless substances in vivo, and their toxic side effects on major organs could be ignored. Therefore, it was inferred that Q-CQDs are low in vivo toxicity carbon nanomaterial for biomedical application.

All in vivo experimental results showed that the prepared Q-CQDs were effective in the treatment of wounds infected with S. aureus, E. coli and S. aureus-co-E. coli through killing bacteria, reduced inflammation and promoting infected-wounds healing, while the cytotoxic effects of Q-CQDs on major organs of mice are negligible. Based on these results, the prepared Q-CQDs expanded the scope and application of CQDs. Therefore, CQDs with antibacterial activity can play roles in antibacterial applications.

In summary, the Q-CQDs contains -OH, -OCH3 and -N+(CH3)3 groups and possess excellent broad-spectrum antibacterial activity. The Q-CQDs functionalized with -N+(CH3)3 had strong adherence behavior on the bacterial cell membrane. The bacterial cells lost their integrity. The permeation of Q-CQDs into microorganisms caused ROS generation and the efflux of cytoplasmic DNA and RNA, leading to the death of bacteria. Q-CQDs were effective in treating infected-wounds with S. aureus, E. coli and mixed S. aureus-co-E. coli through killing bacteria and promoting infected-wounds healing. What's more, the bacterial resistance of Q-CQDs was not observed, Q-CQDs did not cause hemolysis, and Q-CQDs had good biocompatibility in vivo and in vitro toxicity tests. In short, Q-CQDs killed the quartet (broad-spectrum inhibition of various bacteria), specialized in elite (especial to deal with MRSA), suppressed the evil and support the good (very low mammalian cell toxicity and organ toxicity, good therapeutic efficacy in mice infection models). The results suggested that the Q-CQDs are a potential antibacterial candidate for clinical infected-wound healing applications and even bacteria resistant infections. The Q-CQDs represents a non-toxic nano-antibiotic for reversing bacterial resistance and has great potential prospect of clinical application. To broaden the application of Q-CQDs and meet the specific requirements, surface engineering, including tailoring surface functional group coating and subsequent chemical modification, is an effective strategy for further functionalization of Q-CQDs. This work also provides a potential strategy for the especially development of CQDs-based drug delivery systems for effective disease treatment.
